violations.rule: Write Scenarios in BDD/Gherkin Form — Scenario/Given/When/Then forces state → action → outcome and surfaces ambiguity while it's cheap to close
violations.why: Enter-key behavior branches on slash-menu-open state and on which modifier key is held, but is described only as prose bullets, never as Given/When/Then scenarios — exactly the structure that would have forced the missing streaming/empty-draft guard to be decided explicitly instead of left implicit.

violations.rule: Handle errors explicitly, never swallow them; every boundary a design introduces needs stated error handling
violations.why: The new Cmd+Enter/Ctrl+Enter path is specified as calling event.preventDefault() then handleSend() on event.metaKey || event.ctrlKey alone, without restating the !isStreaming && draft.trim() guard the current Enter-to-send path enforces (AssistantPanel.tsx:164); as written it could send an empty/whitespace draft or fire while the assistant is streaming.

notes: Sizing values ('~2 rows', '~8 rows') are approximate rather than named constants — low risk, noted not cited.
Testing section leaves resize-logic extraction as conditional ('if extracted...') rather than a firm decision — test-authoring detail, not a behavior gap.
AssistantPanel.tsx already 454 lines (over the ~400-line soft preference) before this change; still well under the 800-line hard cap.
writing-secure-code skill read and judged not applicable — change is UI/input-mechanics only, SSE/model-call boundary and backend explicitly out of scope in the spec.
No diagram included; judged unnecessary under YAGNI for a small single-file, no-new-dependency change, consistent with sibling specs of the same scope.
All line-number and behavior citations in the spec (AssistantPanel.tsx:161-165, :285-292, placeholder text, slashQuery regex, SLASH_ACTIONS fill pattern) verified accurate against the live file.
Sizing values ('~2 rows', '~8 rows') are approximate rather than named constants — low risk, noted not cited.
Testing section leaves resize-logic extraction as conditional ('if extracted...') rather than a firm decision — test-authoring detail, not a behavior gap.
AssistantPanel.tsx is 454 lines before this change (over the ~400-line soft preference); still well under the 800-line hard cap.
writing-secure-code skill read and judged not applicable — change is UI/input-mechanics only, SSE/model-call boundary and backend explicitly out of scope in the spec.

Round 1's two violations (missing Given/When/Then for §3 Enter-key branching; missing !isStreaming && draft.trim() guard on the Cmd/Ctrl+Enter path) are both resolved: §3 now has four explicit Given/When/Then scenarios, and the fourth restates the guard and its newline-insertion fallback, matched by an updated Testing section.

All line-number and behavior citations in the spec (AssistantPanel.tsx:161-165, :172, :285-292, placeholder text, slashQuery regex, SLASH_ACTIONS fill pattern) re-verified accurate against the live file at this HEAD.

Toolchain (S8) names existing deps at major-version granularity rather than exact pins; acceptable because the spec introduces zero new dependencies and reuses versions already pinned in repo lockfiles.
S4 offers 'drop the underscore or re-export' for _authoritative_events; prefer re-export to honour extend-not-alter for the shared scoring module (scoring.py:119 calls it internally).
S5.1 uses Python round() (banker's rounding); per-queen pick_pct need not sum to 100 — deterministic but worth an implementer comment.

violations.rule: Pin Exact Versions — "an unpinned dependency is a time machine... Pin the exact version of every library and tool"
violations.why: Only @supabase/supabase-js is exact; react/react-dom (^19.2.1), vitest (^4.1.10), @testing-library/react (^16.3.2), @testing-library/user-event (^14.6.1) and typescript (^5.9.3) are caret ranges, and ^19.2.1 has already drifted from the installed tree (package-lock.json resolves React to 19.2.7).

violations.rule: KISS, DRY, YAGNI; prefer the simplest solution that fully solves the problem
violations.why: The mitigation adds a second source of truth for one fact (session.user.new_email ?? pendingEmail) plus a dedicated test, guarding a LOW-rated hypothesis that §4.2 already asserts cannot occur, when the spec's own §10.1 pattern of verifying first would settle it without the redundant branch.

violations.rule: Good, bad, and edge-case scenarios — "anything you leave implicit, the agent infers, and inference is where the defects come from"
violations.why: §4.2 types session.user.email as string | undefined, but §5.1's "Signed in as" line and §7's same-as-current comparison consume it unconditionally, and §8 enumerates only supabase === null and session === null, leaving the undefined-email branch to inference.

notes: §9's CSP row states unconditionally that the Supabase host is already in connect-src; that holds only for the web build (vite.config.web.ts + build/csp.ts rewrite from VITE_SUPABASE_URL). The Electron baseline in src/renderer/index.html is `connect-src 'self' http://localhost:8000` with no Supabase origin. Not a regression (SignInScreen/AuthProvider already operate under it) but broader than what §11.2's dev:web-only manual gate verifies.

§10.2 vs §4.3 wiring is unstated: runAuthAction returns Feedback | null and its AuthResult type discards data, so a literal reading leaves no path to read data.user.new_email. Implementable via the caller's action closure, but never said. Moot if the §10.2 violation is resolved by deletion.
Re-submitting an email change while one is already pending has no scenario in §7; allow/warn/block is left to inference.
The generic throw message 'Something went wrong. Please try again.' is asserted by §6.1 and T-07 but absent from §5.3's copy table, which claims to hold every user-visible string.

DRY on the duplicated run() helper (§4.3) is correctly handled, not a violation: cost quantified, follow-up recorded in §13, flagged as a reviewer-overturnable judgment call per core-conduct's drive-by-cleanup rule.
File-size and naming conventions met: largest new file ~110 lines; PascalCase components, UPPER_SNAKE_CASE message constants, is-prefixed booleans.

writing-secure-code read and in territory (auth + credentials); no violations to cite. No hardcoded secrets, no new model/DB/prompt path, no dangerouslySetInnerHTML, no IDOR surface; the server-issued nonce is a strict improvement over the current sign-out-and-reset path.

Independently verified against the codebase and all held: SignInScreen.tsx:25-41 run() helper, :120-127 feedback roles, :95-99 noValidate rationale, ProfileScreen.tsx:146 session guard and the fieldset/Avatar insertion point, all 11 Tailwind tokens, auth/validation.ts strings, and the nonce/new_email/reauthenticate typings in the installed @supabase/auth-js.
Four user-locked brainstorm decisions (nonce over current-password, neutral email copy, both flows in scope, per-component file layout) treated as approved intent and not re-litigated.
Repo layer: no .claude/project-standards.md exists in mtg-wizard; the repo CLAUDE.md security review checklist was applied and §9 addresses every row.

§3's architecture table still says AccountSection 'returns null in bypass mode' though there are now three null-return conditions (§8.1, §8.2, §8.6); the §8 sections are authoritative and unambiguous, the table summary is just stale.

§7 specifies trimming for the same-as-current comparison but never says whether updateUser({email}) receives the raw or trimmed value; worst case is Supabase rejecting a padded address and rendering the error inline.
No 'updateUser throws' scenario in the email flow (§7); behavior is still fully determined by §4.3's helper contract, so it is an untested path rather than an unspecified one.
§8.2 says ProfileScreen.tsx:146 guards 'the same way', but that line uses disabled={session === null} rather than a null return; the precedent invoked is real, the phrasing is imprecise.

Scope item 4's backlog bookkeeping (moving the shipped assistant-chat-input idea out of ideas.md Unprocessed) belongs to PR #24 rather than this feature; declared in scope and docs-only, so a reviewer preference call rather than a drive-by-cleanup violation.

Verified independently at this HEAD: all 8 §12 versions match apps/desktop/node_modules exactly; npm run test -- --run yields 277 passed across 35 files as §11.1 claims; SignInScreen.test.tsx has 11 tests; index.html CSP baseline has no Supabase origin and build/csp.ts + vite.config.web.ts exist as §9 describes.

Round-1 notes still open and still non-blocking: §5.3's copy table omits the generic throw message 'Something went wrong. Please try again.', and §7 has no scenario for re-submitting an email while a change is already pending.

violations.rule: API contracts give the agent real interface boundaries instead of letting it improvise shapes other components then fail to match
violations.why: The launcher-to-judge prompt template is never specified, and the validated arg set cannot carry inputs both agent definitions document as required: compliance's context summary and prior-round violations, observability's decisions summary.

violations.rule: Persistent violations escalate to the user, never silently waived
violations.where: §6.2 hooks/spec-guard.sh decision order and revise loop
violations.why: Moving the revise loop into a hook with no cross-invocation attempt state drops the skill's escalation tripwires (same id in two consecutive rounds, round-3 cap) and names no new owner for them.

violations.rule: KISS, DRY, YAGNI - prefer the simplest solution that fully solves the problem
violations.why: Five spawn rungs are committed to when the stated need is met by the measured rung plus headless; spike S2 concedes the env-var selection mechanism is unmeasured, and rungs 3-4 are the sole reason the osascript injection surface must be designed around.

violations.rule: Many small, focused files (<400 lines, 800 max) over few large ones
violations.why: One script is scoped to carry arg validation, preflight, run-dir/manifest creation, run.sh generation, a five-rung ladder, full lock lifecycle, and poll/liveness/exit mapping, with no decomposition or size budget stated, in a repo whose largest hook is 211 lines.

coding-memory/judge-runs/ stores frozen prompts, full model output, stderr and argv but is given only a gitignore entry and no permission posture, so the 'No secrets in run dirs' invariant has no mechanism behind it.

notes: Launcher exit code 2 is never defined (0,1,3,4,5 used consistently); presumably reserved to avoid collision with the hook's exit 2, but left to inference.
round = max(stored round) + 1 is undefined for a spec with no stored verdicts; natural reading is round 1 but it is inferred.
.gitignore currently has no coding-memory/judge-runs/ entry and §12's documentation obligations omit adding one.
Verified accurate: claude 2.1.215, bash 3.2.57(1), python 3.9.6, tmux 3.6a, git 2.50.1; both agents' tool lists match §4.1 exactly.
The --bare amendment is handled correctly: flagged at the top, justified in §4.2, gated behind blocking spike S1 - a human-owned change surfaced rather than silently decided.
A git commit can block up to 840s; §6.5 explains the timeout ordering but the operator-facing cost is not called out for the user's review gate.

violations.rule: API contracts give the agent real interface boundaries instead of letting it improvise shapes other components then fail to match
violations.where: §6.1.2 argument contract; §6.1.3 prompt template; §6.2 decision order
violations.why: The hook path never specifies how it produces the mandatory --context-file (or §6.3's --decisions-file), and the prompt template has no slot for observability's --spec or --test-cmd-file, so a deterministic launch cannot satisfy the launcher's own required-argument contract.

violations.rule: Persistent violations escalate to the user, never silently waived
violations.where: §6.2.1 round accounting and the escalation cap
violations.why: The 'same id in the two most recent rounds' tripwire depends on the judge reusing ids, which requires the prior round's violations array in the prompt, yet nothing specifies the hook building --prior-violations-file from the store, so that half of the cap can never fire.

violations.rule: Pin the exact version of every library and tool the spec names
violations.where: §4 pinned toolchain table; §6.1 terminal ladder rung 1
violations.why: cmux is the ladder's first and primary rung but is absent from the pinned table that covers every other named tool, and its spawn is specified only as 'cmux pane' with no command, leaving the highest-priority rung unimplementable.

notes: Round 1's core-conduct/small-focused-files, core-conduct/default-deny-stores, and core-conduct/yagni are all cleanly closed: six libs with harness-enforced line budgets, umask 077 + 0700/0600 with post-creation stat assertions failing preflight, and a four-rung ladder each rung of which maps to a terminal the user actually uses (user decision, recorded).
§5.2 is placed before §5.1 in document order - cosmetic, but a reader following section numbers will stumble.

The git global-option pass-through (-C, -c, --git-dir, --work-tree, --namespace, --exec-path, --config-env) is notable surface plus four test cases for a gate whose stated need is spec commits in this repo, and §6.2 already accepts fail-open-with-a-warning for unclassifiable invocations which would cover -C at zero cost. Not cited as YAGNI because `git -C ~/.claude commit` is a plausible real pattern here, but worth a KISS pass.

All four of round 1's notes are addressed: exit code 2 explicitly reserved and explained, empty-store round defined as 1, .gitignore in §12 with an ordering constraint, and §6.7 states the operator-facing 840s cost.

§6.5's handling of spike S3 is exemplary - an unmeasured assumption named as blocking, with the fallback design fork spelled out and reserved for measurement rather than implementation-time improvisation.
Growth from 464 to ~855 lines is substance, not padding: §5.2 (two-hash livelock), §6.1.1 (decomposition), §6.2.1 (escalation cap), and §6.5 (S3 fork) are all load-bearing.
The revision self-reported a defect found during revision (gate and judge hashing different content) and fixed it rather than deferring - good practice.

Verified independently: observability-judge.md's declared inputs are stage, decisions summary, optional spec path, test command, base branch - confirming the template's missing slots; cmux is a real binary on this machine (/Applications/cmux.app/Contents/Resources/bin, invoked elsewhere in this repo as ${CMUX_CLAUDE_HOOK_CMUX_BIN:-cmux}), so the missing pin is fillable, not unknowable.

violations.rule: API contracts give the agent real interface boundaries instead of letting it improvise shapes other components then fail to match
violations.where: §6.2.1 argument-provisioning table; §5.1 run-id layout; §6.1.2 --*-file validation; §6.1.3 prompt template
violations.why: The hook is told to write --prior-violations-file into <run-dir>/prior-violations.json, but the run dir is created inside the launcher under a run-id containing the launcher's own PID and every --*-file must already exist to pass validation, so the one argument that makes the escalation cap fire cannot be produced by its specified caller.

notes: §6.1.2 points at §6.2.2 for the optional-input fallbacks, but they are in §6.2.1; §6.2.2 is round accounting.

§6.1.3's template specifies an absent-form for test_command and waived but not for design_doc, and §6.2.1 never covers observability's --spec on the judge-guard path; non-blocking because that input is declared optional.
§3's flowchart still shows the unconditional staged==worktree precondition that §5.2 disproves and scopes to plain git commit, and omits the per-form detection step.
§10's case table is still headed 'Cases the round-2 revisions add' while listing round-3 cases.

Round 2's writing-specs/pinned-versions and gates/escalation-not-preserved are properly closed: cmux pinned 0.64.20 (100) with a full rung-1 command, and §6.2.1 derives every hook-side argument from repo and store.

Independently re-ran §5.2's git claims against real git (commit -a with a never-staged spec, pathspec commit, brand-new staged spec, untracked-file pathspec commit); the per-form table is empirically correct on every row.
Declining --context-file on the hook path is a security improvement: it stops the spec's author from also authoring the standard it is judged against.
YAGNI clean against the stated need; the four-rung ladder is a settled user decision and rung 4 is the correctness floor.

Security clean: run dirs default-deny with post-creation mode assertion, run.sh indirection covers both interpolation points including rung 1's --command, no secrets or absolute paths in the artifact.

writing-specs/api-contracts (rounds 1-3) is closed: no caller provisions prior violations; the launcher derives them from the store after creating the run dir, so every argument on every caller path (hook/compliance, hook/observability, skill, ad-hoc override) is producible by that caller at call time.
Vestigial validation branch: --*-file may resolve 'inside repo or run dir', but no caller can pre-place a file in a run dir only the launcher creates; dead branch worth pruning at implementation.

Unstated misuse edge: ad-hoc --round > 1 against a spec with no stored rounds leaves the extraction result unspecified; unreachable on both specified caller paths, covered in spirit by the fail-closed posture (section 9.5), one sentence would close it.

Dry-run invocation mechanism implicit: the hook should be stated to re-execute the parsed argv list (python subprocess), never re-joined shell text; not a security hole (same trust domain) but re-quoting could misclassify.
A literal 'git commit --dry-run' run by the agent is itself gated; over-block toward fail-closed, consistent with stated posture, unenumerated in section 7.

Spec is ~1096 lines vs core-conduct's 800-line ceiling; section 11 names the breach and defers the split to the user at review — surfaced human-owned decision, recorded for the review gate, not cited as a violation.

All four round-3 notes fixed: section 3 flowchart shows dry-run detection and the universal precondition; 6.1.2 fallback pointer corrected to 6.2.1; design_doc has an absent-form; section 10 heading matches contents.
Section 6.5 timeout correction is honest in the risk-increasing direction (10 of 17 hooks set timeouts, all 10s; 900s is a 90x extrapolation) and S3 correctly stays blocking.

Detection rewrite (git commit --dry-run --porcelain -z, universal staged==worktree precondition) deletes the per-form analysis where both prior detection bugs lived; KISS-sound, backed by mandated real-git falsification tests.

violations.rule: No requirement readable two ways; anything left implicit the agent infers, and inference is where the defects come from
violations.why: The spec_unit block's location is specified only as 'a fenced yaml block near the top' and the root file contains three such blocks — including §5.3's own example declaring part_of the root itself — so unit resolution is undefined for the design's own first instance and the undefined cases fall through to the fail-open standalone row.

violations.rule: API contracts must give the agent the real interface boundaries to build against instead of letting it improvise shapes other components then fail to match
violations.where: §4.1 and §2 scope table vs. §6.1.3 prompt contract and §12
violations.why: §4.1 and §2 commit to exactly one added declared input (spec_unit_sha) and bar any other agent-definition change, while §6.1.3 and §12 require the judge to also receive and act on unit_members, and §6.2.2 asserts as an invariant that the agent has no undeclared field to interpret.

notes: §5.3's bidirectional-consistency bullet is stated one-directionally (companion->root) and never fires when resolution starts from the root; the companion's header states it symmetrically and the distinct-roots refusal covers the both-files-committed case, so the gap is narrow.
Depth-1 nesting has a §5.3 rule, a §8 failure row and a §10 test case but no §7 Gherkin scenario, unlike its sibling one-sided part_of failure.
The split is well executed: 684 + 696 lines, both under core-conduct's 800 max, numbering deliberately continuous, and every cross-file section reference verified to resolve in both directions.
The compliance-store change is genuinely additive and nullable, with §5's fallback key preserving existing behaviour and §10 asserting backward compatibility against a pre-existing stored row.

§10's unit coverage is otherwise strong including the companion-only-edit case and its falsification target; the gaps track violation 1 exactly (no multiple/ambiguous declaration block case, none for an unparseable block).

Unchanged and still clean: §4's six pinned machine-verified tool versions, §5.1/§9's default-deny run-dir posture asserted after creation, the run.sh indirection keeping prompt text off every rung's command line, no secrets or absolute paths, S1/S3 held as blocking spikes.
YAGNI holds: the spec-unit model is forced by the user-directed split, not speculative.
